license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: WSMan session initialized
entries.summary: A WinRM/WSMan session was created on this host.
entries.details: Generated on the destination host when a WSMan (WinRM) session is successfully initialized. This is the primary inbound WinRM connection indicator. Key fields: connection URI (identifies the endpoint being accessed, e.g., wsman://hostname/wsman) and the user context. WinRM is the transport layer for PowerShell remoting (Enter-PSSession, Invoke-Command), remote CIM/WMI operations, and tools such as evil-winrm. An inbound EID 6 on a non-management host from an unexpected source is a strong lateral movement indicator. Correlate with Security EID 4624 LogonType 3 (network logon) on the same host to confirm authentication.

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4624 LogonType 3 using the same timestamp and username to confirm the network logon
Connection URI identifies the WinRM endpoint — wsman:// connections to non-standard endpoints may indicate tool use
Correlate with PowerShell EID 4103/4104 to review commands executed over the session
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate remote administration via PowerShell remoting from IT or admin hosts
Ansible, DSC, or other configuration management tools using WinRM

entries.title: WSMan session deinitialized
entries.summary: A WinRM/WSMan session was closed on this host.
entries.details: Generated when an established WSMan session ends. Correlate with EID 6 using the session identifier to calculate session duration. A session that was opened (EID 6) but shows no corresponding EID 8 may indicate an abnormally terminated or still-active connection. Session duration can help distinguish interactive lateral movement (longer sessions) from automated one-shot command execution (very short sessions).

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 6 to calculate session duration
Very short sessions (sub-second) are typical of Invoke-Command one-liners; longer sessions indicate interactive use
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate WinRM session closing normally

entries.title: WSMan session created
entries.summary: A WinRM shell session was created on this host.
entries.details: Generated when a WSMan shell is created within an established WinRM session. Represents the creation of an interactive or scripted shell environment over WinRM, as opposed to just a transport session (EID 6). Tools such as evil-winrm and PowerShell's Enter-PSSession will generate this event. Key field: the ResourceUri, which indicates the type of shell created (e.g., http://schemas.microsoft.com/wbem/wsman/1/windows/shell/cmd for a command shell, or a PowerShell endpoint URI).

investigationPivots: ResourceUri identifies the shell type — a cmd shell URI from an unexpected host warrants immediate investigation
Correlate with PowerShell EID 4103/4104 if a PowerShell endpoint URI is present to review executed commands
Pair with EID 6 to link the shell session back to the originating WSMan session
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate PowerShell remoting sessions from authorized admin hosts
Automation tools creating WinRM shells for remote task execution

entries.title: WSMan user authentication succeeded
entries.details: Generated on the destination host when a user successfully authenticates to WinRM. Key fields: the authenticated username and authentication mechanism (e.g., Kerberos, NTLM, Basic, CredSSP). CredSSP authentication is particularly noteworthy as it delegates full credentials to the remote host, making it a target for credential theft. NTLM authentication in environments that should use Kerberos may indicate pass-the-hash or a non-domain-joined source. Correlate with EID 6 and Security EID 4624 to build the full authentication and session picture.

investigationPivots: Authentication mechanism is a key pivot: CredSSP delegates credentials and is higher risk; NTLM where Kerberos is expected may indicate pass-the-hash
Correlate username with Security EID 4624 LogonType 3 to confirm the network logon record
Unexpected accounts (service accounts, disabled accounts, non-admin users) authenticating via WinRM warrant investigation
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate admin authentication via PowerShell remoting
Service accounts used by configuration management tools
